passages.explanation: The Passover and the Festival of Unleavened Bread are only two days away. The chief priests and the teachers of the law are scheming to arrest Jesus secretly and kill him. But not during the festival, they say — or the people may riot. The authorities want Jesus dead but need to manage public opinion. They need a way to arrest him quietly, away from the festival crowds. Judas will provide exactly that.

While Jesus is in Bethany, reclining at the table in the home of Simon the Leper, a woman comes with an alabaster jar of very expensive perfume — pure nard. She breaks the jar and pours the perfume on his head. Some present are indignant: why this waste of perfume? It could have been sold for more than a year's wages and the money given to the poor. They rebuke her harshly. Jesus says: leave her alone. Why are you bothering her? She's done a beautiful thing to me. The poor you will always have with you, and you can help them any time you want. But you will not always have me. She did what she could — she poured perfume on my body beforehand to prepare for my burial. Truly I tell you, wherever the gospel is preached throughout the world, what she has done will also be told, in memory of her. The woman understands something the disciples don't — Jesus is about to die. Her extravagant act is anointing for burial. The critics see waste; Jesus sees worship. And he immortalises her anonymous act in the story of the gospel itself.

Then Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve, goes to the chief priests to betray Jesus to them. They're delighted to hear this and promise to give him money. So he watches for an opportunity to hand Jesus over. Mark places Judas' betrayal immediately after the anointing — the contrast is stark. A woman spends a year's wages on Jesus. Judas sells Jesus for money. Two responses to the same person, in the same week.

On the first day of the Festival of Unleavened Bread, when it's customary to sacrifice the Passover lamb, the disciples ask: where do you want us to go and make preparations for you to eat the Passover? He sends two disciples: go into the city, and a man carrying a jar of water will meet you. Follow him and say to the owner of the house: the Teacher asks, where is my guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples? He will show you a large room upstairs, furnished and ready. Make preparations there. The disciples leave, go into the city, find things just as Jesus said, and prepare the Passover. When evening comes, Jesus arrives with the Twelve. While they're reclining and eating, Jesus says: truly I tell you, one of you will betray me — one who is eating with me. They're saddened and one by one say: surely you don't mean me? He replies: it is one of the Twelve — one who dips bread into the bowl with me. The Son of Man will go just as it is written about him. But woe to that man who betrays the Son of Man! It would be better for him if he had not been born. The betrayal comes from within the intimate circle — from someone sharing the same dish. Jesus knows and proceeds anyway. The 'woe' isn't a curse; it's a lament.

While they're eating, Jesus takes bread, gives thanks, breaks it, and gives it to his disciples: take it — this is my body. Then he takes a cup, gives thanks, and offers it to them, and they all drink from it. He says: this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many. Truly I tell you, I will not drink again from the fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new in the kingdom of God. When they've sung a hymn, they go out to the Mount of Olives. The Passover commemorated Israel's liberation from Egypt through the blood of a lamb. Jesus transforms it: the bread is his body, the wine is his blood. The new covenant is established not by animal sacrifice but by his death. 'Poured out for many' echoes Isaiah 53. And the promise of drinking new wine in the kingdom says: this isn't the end of the story.

Jesus tells them: you will all fall away, for it is written — I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered. But after I have risen, I will go ahead of you into Galilee. Peter declares: even if all fall away, I will not. Jesus answers: truly I tell you, today — yes, tonight — before the rooster crows twice you yourself will disown me three times. But Peter insists emphatically: even if I have to die with you, I will never disown you. And all the others say the same. Jesus quotes Zechariah 13:7 — the scattering of the disciples is prophesied, not accidental. He also promises reunion after resurrection: I'll go ahead of you to Galilee. But Peter can't hear the promise because he's too busy protesting the prediction.

They go to a place called Gethsemane. Jesus says: sit here while I pray. He takes Peter, James, and John along and begins to be deeply distressed and troubled. He tells them: my soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death. Stay here and keep watch. Going a little farther, he falls to the ground and prays that if possible the hour might pass from him: Abba, Father, everything is possible for you. Take this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will. He returns and finds them sleeping. He says to Peter: Simon, are you asleep? Couldn't you keep watch for one hour? Watch and pray so that you won't fall into temptation. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak. He goes away and prays the same thing. Again he comes back and finds them sleeping — they don't know what to say. He comes the third time: are you still sleeping and resting? Enough! The hour has come. Look, the Son of Man is delivered into the hands of sinners. Rise! Let's go! Here comes my betrayer! Mark's Gethsemane is raw and unsparing. Jesus is distressed, troubled, overwhelmed with sorrow. He addresses God as 'Abba' — the most intimate Aramaic term for father. He asks for another way and accepts there isn't one. Three times he prays, three times the disciples fail to stay awake. Their failure foreshadows Peter's three denials.

Just as Jesus is speaking, Judas, one of the Twelve, appears with a crowd armed with swords and clubs, sent by the chief priests, teachers of the law, and elders. The betrayer has arranged a signal: the one I kiss is the man — arrest him and lead him away under guard. Going at once to Jesus, Judas says: Rabbi! — and kisses him. The men seize Jesus and arrest him. One of those standing nearby draws his sword and strikes the servant of the high priest, cutting off his ear. Jesus says: am I leading a rebellion, that you've come out with swords and clubs to capture me? Every day I was with you, teaching in the temple courts, and you didn't arrest me. But the Scriptures must be fulfilled. Then everyone deserts him and flees. A young man wearing nothing but a linen garment follows Jesus. When they seize him, he flees naked, leaving his garment behind. The kiss is the ultimate perversion of intimacy. Everyone flees — exactly as predicted. The mysterious young man who flees naked is unique to Mark; many scholars believe it's Mark himself, inserting a personal detail. The naked flight symbolises the total abandonment and shame of the moment.

They take Jesus to the high priest, and all the chief priests, the elders, and the teachers of the law assemble. Peter follows at a distance, right into the courtyard of the high priest. He sits with the guards and warms himself at the fire. The chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin look for evidence against Jesus to put him to death, but they can't find any. Many testify falsely against him, but their statements don't agree. Some stand up and give false testimony: we heard him say 'I will destroy this temple made with human hands and in three days build another, not made with hands.' Even their testimony doesn't agree. The high priest stands up and asks: are you not going to answer? What is this testimony? But Jesus remains silent. Again the high priest asks: are you the Messiah, the Son of the Blessed One? Jesus says: I am. And you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Mighty One and coming on the clouds of heaven. The high priest tears his clothes: why do we need any more witnesses? You've heard the blasphemy. What do you think? They all condemn him as worthy of death. Some begin to spit at him. They blindfold him, strike him, and say: prophesy! The guards beat him. In Mark's account, Jesus breaks his silence with the most direct messianic claim in the Gospel: 'I am.' The high priest gets his blasphemy charge, but Jesus has told the truth. The irony of 'prophesy!' — shouted by people who are fulfilling prophecy — is devastating.

While Peter is below in the courtyard, one of the high priest's servant girls comes. She sees Peter warming himself, looks closely at him: you also were with that Nazarene, Jesus. He denies it: I don't know or understand what you're talking about. He goes out into the entryway, and the rooster crows. The servant girl sees him there and again tells those standing around: this fellow is one of them. Again he denies it. After a little while, those standing near say: surely you're one of them, for you are a Galilean. He begins to call down curses and swears: I don't know this man you're talking about! Immediately the rooster crows the second time. Then Peter remembers what Jesus told him: before the rooster crows twice you will disown me three times. And he breaks down and weeps. Three denials, each more vehement than the last — the final one includes cursing and swearing. Peter doesn't just distance himself; he invokes oaths against himself if he's lying. The man who said he'd die for Jesus won't even admit knowing him. The rooster crows and the memory hits. Mark's account is likely Peter's own testimony — no one tells this story more harshly than the person who lived it.
